item.content:encoded: After three years my first SCP has finally hit +100.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Terrifying to consider as a anomaly and also a well written skip.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Today I really think I was partially wrong about this skip. At most I would move some of the technical stuff to footnotes to make the text less tiring to read, but otherwise it is fairly ok to me.

My thoughts on SCP-2378, though, haven't changed nearly as much. But now if I still think you could make this into a supplement isn't it because I think this isn't material for its own entry, anymore, but instead because I think you could make more entries about specially anomalous organisms found inside 2378 as this one was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this overall but the last bit didn't really do it for me. I was hoping for more detail on what the Foundation was doing with the proteins. Why did they decide to select for color, and not, say size or shape or number of flagella? Do they just think they're pretty?

I did get a kick out of the experimenters consistently getting too freaked out to continue after ten minutes.

If they mutate wildly whenever they come into contact with foreign DNA, why didn't they react to the walls of their original environment or the researcher they first attacked?

Nevermind, I reread the article. Still not clear on what "transformation procedures" are, but I'm guessing from the lack of in-article description that it's a standard bacterial thing I should already know about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is definitely one of the more skips I've seen, in my opinion it's creative and a little disturbing. I did enjoy seeing how much detail there was, especially the explanation of the Square Cube Law. +1 from me.

item.title: Re: Complementing what I said in SCP-2378
item.content:encoded:
So, I've seen you post on both 2946, and here and I wanted to follow up a bit with you cause you do give some good feedback, albeit I don't entirely agree with everything being said. To your first part, probably one of the bigger reasons this and 2378 has succeeded is due to the technical detail and painstaking accuracy of those details. Perhaps they are a little over the top, but I have seen SCPs concerning physics, archaeology, terminology, and even math with as much detail and attention to the material as I've done here. The important thing to understand about this piece is that it is not written for a biologist. The terminology, the technical detail and the language is written in such a way that an average person could read it and understand what is going on. In it's original form, the criticism would very much have fit the bill, as there was a whole host of jargon and terminology that made it impossible for non-biology focused persons to have read the article. In it's current iteration? It's nowhere near as bad.

Point number 2 is fair, and something I really should move to the containment procedures.

Point number 3 I strongly disagree with. It's pretty clear, based on the nature of 2378, that 2946 is a distinct anomaly, because unlike other components of 2378 it has distinct components and attributes that set it apart. Even more important is the fact that the Foundation has removed instances from the initial anomaly, which no longer makes them solely attached to the original entity, meaning that they do in fact require separate containment procedures and files.

item.title: Complementing what I said in SCP-2378
item.content:encoded:
This is good, be sure of it. I have, again, some serious issues, but the idea is good, and the execution just needs trimming.

1- Too dry. The problem isn't that you didn't trimmed it enough, but maybe that you approach it the "wrong" way. You try to be the less dry possible while doing a complete biological description meant for a biology researcher.

But the main entry of an SCP is a document meant for presentation of the anomalies involved and how to keep the containment, meant for personnel of various distinct professions. Too specific elements should be moved to related documents in-universe, just cited about on the main entry is they are not needed for the fiction.

You are trying to talk to biologists and to deliver every detail about it in a presentation document meant even for the cleaning crew. Not quite, but something in this direction.

2- The collapsible with further Containment procedures should be at the Containment Procedures section. It isn't bad, but it is completely out of place. Even if it seems to follow up a piece of description, there is a reason for the Procedure to be distinct and in the beginning.

3- My opinion, and I repeat it. This entry will work wonderfully as a supplement to SCP-2378. Even if it have a distinct procedure for being kept in laboratory, as an Anomaly Containment it is just a furthering of SCP-2378. It is a distinct entity, sure, but still a related event with related Containment. The first definition for a Containment Procedure isn't the procedure to keep it in a lab, but the procedure to keep it away from the public, and in this, it is jus a part of the containment of the giant intestine.

On a side note, I think you can improve both entries through the further use of collapsible entries. If you want to keep the fine descriptions of the biological processes, that seem to be part of the novelty of the skips, you can take then out of the "main" text and put then in collapsible text. That way, you can further clean up the text without getting rid of the detailed descriptions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for the detailed, clear and thorough containment procedures as much as the article itself. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bingo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was the containment incident before the glucose-intake-limiting procedure for SCP-2378 was enacted? Why didn't 2946 detect the glucose in the snacks prior to Dr. Blankbox ingesting them? I'd think they'd go after Dr. Blankbox's lunchbox far before she had a chance to break out a Snickers.

EDIT: Wait, it has to do with the fructose and/or sucrose having to turn into glucose inside her body over that ten-minute time gap, isn't it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cheetos wat kill you. Yeah

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point, I meant to come in and change that link.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
exploring SCP ████

Suggestion: crosslink this to SCP-2378 now that it exists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The photo looks like a bunch of neon-colored Cheetos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The experiment log is still titled "Experiment Logs E-XXXX-13"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The writhing mass of cells swarmed her

Tone here (and in a few other areas, the word "bizarrely" jumped out at me) is a bit informal at points.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As discussed in chat, I like this even thogh it is very long :)
You get +1 for all the dumb questions I asked

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. I like the way strung things out so it wasn't immediately obvious, and the hints that this might have been intentionally constructed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've definitely gotten this reaction from some, the "jargon" is actually quite narrowed from what it was, and it took a great amount of work to get it written in such a way that it…wasn't so jargony. The original draft was so dense it gave some people headaches.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a great example of "adaptive evolution what kills you" with good descriptive language that just repeatedly grossed me out in the best way.

But this is WAY too dense in the scientific jargon department. I like to think I can really stick with a dry entry to get to the meat of it, but I repeatedly found myself glossing over the many technical descriptions of this ship's biological functions, both in the containment procedures and description; I feel it needs significant paring down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is far too dry for me to get into. There isn't much to be excited about beyond some admittedly well-constructed science jargon. The first collapsible was a disappointingly pointless. Any essential information there could have been brought into the SCProcedures or easily shoved off into 'another document' (ie: referencing a nonexistent file). Kate McTiriss is one of my favorite authors to use this, by citing non-existent internal Foundation publications and their authors.

The final reveal was rather dull. Though each test displayed a different sample, each followed basically the same format of 'hyper evolves, becomes dangerous, is dead'. The hyper-evolution angle may have been more entertaining if we didn't already have SCP-407 and SCP-1859, doubly so if anything of note happened. I feel cheated reading the same information repeatedly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In that case, you seem to really know what you're talking about. Very impressive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Woah, I saw this as a draft in chat, and now its here!
Glad to see its doing great!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Macromicrobiology without it being a human predator? Good show. The last time someone tried to do macrobacteria, it was highly aggressive and nearly unstoppable. This is much better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will admit that I am usually resistant to 'it kills you' SCPs, but as Praetor Avacyn pointed out, this does it well. There's a squick factor to 'giant anomalous bacterial cells' to begin with, but the experimental logs push it well into nightmare fuel territory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *Blushes* Awwww shucks, that means a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most of the proofreading was for tone, condensation and grammar, and what you see here is actually a heavily condensed final product from the complex and jargon heavy original entry. Either way, I appreciate the upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well…the sex sure would be something, that much is for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It took time and a lot of effort, lots of rewrites, and a whole lot of feedback and input but it paid off in the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome concept and brilliant execution. I really enjoyed reading this +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congrats Bleep. I knew you'd make it to the manliest eventually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a science nerd, I would like to marry this skip. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is fantastic, because it makes you question yourself why this has never been done before.
Well maybe it has, but you're doing it well.

Good thing that you got it proofread as well, you can tell by the detail and the scientific terminology.

Upvote from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. It's an interesting concept that's well rounded and well executed. This is proof you don't need to weave a backstory into your SCP, it needs to be interesting and keeps the reader reading.

item.title: Re: Problems
item.content:encoded: I appreciate this upvote, I'll see if I can't fix the spacing issues.

item.title: Problems
item.content:encoded:
Generic List of Problems

1. Some words don't have spacing between them: completion of protein expression.These drains

2. There appears to be some issues with paragraph spacing but that could just be my screen.

No other issues :) Idea is really good and i thoroughly enjoyed reading it. Let this be a testament to all other people wanting to do a scary scip that causes death as an example of how to do it well!

Upvote from me :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a false color modification of a Scanning Electron Microscope image taken by the NIAID, a sub section of the NIH, and posted on flickr under creative commons. https://www.flickr.com/photos/niaid/16598492368/

Many thanks to minmin for the "Crazy mutation" idea and to Cetus, weryllium, Solo and many others that I'm forgetting at the moment for proofreading and bouncing ideas off of me.
